QF.id: XIII Trading, Clearing, Settlement and Risk Management_76

question.text: A client buys 50 lots of USDINR options (lot size USD 1,000 each) paying a premium of Rs 0.50 per USD. If stamp duty on options is 0.003% of the premium value, what stamp duty is payable?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Premium value = 50 lots x USD 1,000 x Rs 0.50 = Rs 25,000. Stamp duty = 0.003% x Rs 25,000 = Rs 0.75. For options, stamp duty is calculated on the premium value, not the notional value of the contract. **The Trap:** Rs 7.50 uses 0.03% instead of 0.003%. Rs 75 uses 0.3%. Rs 12.53 wrongly calculates stamp duty on the notional value (50 x 1000 x 83.50 x 0.003%). The key distinction is that options stamp duty is on premium, not notional. **Cross-Exam Context:** Stamp duty rates and calculation base differ between futures (on traded value) and options (on premium value). This applies across all derivative segments — Series I, VIII, XVI. The Indian Stamp Act amendments in 2020 standardized electronic stamp duty collection. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Stamp duty on derivatives is very small but adds up for high-frequency traders. Ensure clients factor in all transaction costs — brokerage, exchange fees, clearing fees, GST, stamp duty, and STT (for equity derivatives) — when evaluating trading strategies.
